food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish with grilled eel

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Unagi Don (grilled eel rice bowl). FOODON_03311737 (eel food product) is the closest parent class covering eel-based preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers freshwater eel as an ingredient. No composite dish code exists for Unagi Don in FoodEx2; closest_parent used for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Unagi Don or grilled eel rice bowl as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Unagi Don is approximately 350 g, consisting of a bowl of steamed white rice topped with one or two fillets of kabayaki-style grilled eel and a portion of tamago (Japanese egg).

nutrient_source.title: Unagi (Freshwater Eel, Kabayaki style) and Steamed White Rice — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) — composite calculation from eel kabayaki and cooked white rice components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Unagi Don as a complete dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of: (1) USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175168 — Fish, eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (representing kabayaki-grilled eel, ~120 g per serving); (2) USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169756 — Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (representing steamed rice, ~200 g per serving); (3) tare sauce contribution estimated from standard kabayaki sauce composition (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, sake) at ~30 g per serving. Per-100g values are derived from a 350 g total serving weight. Tamago contribution is minor and folded into the egg component estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice (steamed, Japanese-style) and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Unagi Don as a complete dish. GI is estimated at 50 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese-style steamed white rice, which has a reported GI of approximately 48–56 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The presence of eel protein and fat, tare sauce sugars, and egg moderates the overall glycemic response relative to plain rice. A conservative midpoint GI of 50 is applied. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 78.75) / 100 = 39.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 22.5 × 3.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tare sauce (soy sauce base)
hypertension.guidance: Unagi Don contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1085 mg per standard 350 g serving), primarily from the tare glaze; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation to stay within the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice and tare sauce sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard serving of Unagi Don carries a glycemic load of approximately 39, driven by the white rice base and added sugars in the tare sauce; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion, substituting brown rice where available, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from freshwater eel balanced against sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Freshwater eel provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin D, which are cardioprotective; however, the sodium content from tare sauce is a concern for cardiovascular patients, and portion control combined with reduced-sodium soy sauce in the tare is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Don (鰻丼, also called Unadon) is a classic Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of steamed short-grain white rice topped with fillets of freshwater eel (unagi, Anguilla japonica) that have been prepared in the kabayaki style: the eel is butterflied, skewered, grilled over charcoal, steamed to tenderise, and then re-grilled while being repeatedly basted with a sweet-savoury tare glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The result is eel with a lacquered, caramelised exterior and a soft, rich, fatty interior. In Singapore restaurant contexts, the dish is often served with a portion of tamago (Japanese egg, either tamagoyaki or a soft-boiled egg). The flavour profile is deeply umami, sweet, and smoky, with a melt-in-the-mouth texture from the eel fat. Nutritionally, Unagi Don is a moderate-to-high energy dish providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g. It is a meaningful source of high-quality protein (~8.5 g/100 g), omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D (~3.5 mcg/100 g), vitamin B12 (~1.8 mcg/100 g), and vitamin A (~52 mcg RAE/100 g), making it one of the more micronutrient-dense Japanese rice bowl options. The primary clinical concerns are its moderate-to-high glycemic load from white rice and tare sugars, and its elevated sodium content from the soy-based glaze, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Unagi is considered a delicacy in Japan and is traditionally eaten on Doyo no Ushi no Hi (Midsummer Day of the Ox) for stamina. The dish is widely available in Japanese restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Unagi Don?
Is Unagi Don good for diabetics?
Is eel rice bowl high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a complete composite entry for Unagi Don as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked eel (FDC 175168) and cooked white rice (FDC 169756) with tare sauce contribution estimated from standard kabayaki sauce composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style steamed white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved source entry covers Unagi Don as a complete dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked eel and white rice components; no industrial trans fats are present in traditional kabayaki preparation. 3. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style steamed white rice (GI ~48–56, University of Sydney GI Database); the protein and fat from eel and egg are expected to moderately lower the composite GI relative to plain rice. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for eel and rice; no sentinel -1 values are used as all fields have defensible estimates from approved sources. 5. The input record listed 'Alcoholic Drink' as a cuisine tag — this appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and is not reflected in the food classification. 6. Halal status is false as eel (unagi) is generally considered non-halal in the Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence followed in Singapore, though opinions vary; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 7. Serving size of 350 g is a standard estimate for a Japanese restaurant rice bowl with eel and egg; actual restaurant portions may vary.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch using USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for eel and rice components. Added Japanese local name 鰻丼 and aliases うな丼 and 鰻重. Set GI to 50 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style steamed white rice). Verified gi_category = 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule. Verified all aliases_by_language entries present verbatim in flat aliases list. Verified no -1 sentinel values used (all fields estimated from approved sources). Verified trans_fat_g = 0.0 documented in notes as confirmed zero. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (exact, closest_parent, none). All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria verified as passing.
